skill.name: backend-dev-guidelines
skill.description: Guidance for building Node.js, Express, TypeScript, and Prisma backend services with layered architecture, validation, monitoring, configuration, and testing patterns.
skill.summary: Build consistent Express and Prisma backends with layered TypeScript patterns.
skill.author: Dimon94

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, filesystem, environment, network, and sensitive-data patterns, but review shows they are Markdown documentation examples rather than executable skill behavior. No prompt injection or malicious exfiltration intent was found. The main publish warning is that Sentry examples enable request data and local variable capture, which could expose sensitive runtime data if copied without stronger redaction.

medium_findings.title: Monitoring Examples May Capture Sensitive Runtime Data
medium_findings.description: The Sentry initialization example enables local variable capture and request data collection, including request data, headers, IP address, query string, URL, and user identifiers. The example removes authorization and cookie headers and masks email addresses, but copied code could still send sensitive body fields, other headers, or local variables to Sentry.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is documentation, not executable code, so there is no direct exfiltration by the skill. The risk is still real because the recommended Sentry configuration can collect sensitive runtime data if adopted without stricter redaction.

medium_findings.title: Weak Development Secret Placeholders in Configuration Examples
medium_findings.description: The configuration guide includes placeholder secrets such as a development session secret and a sample database password. The same file also tells users not to commit secrets, so this is a documentation hygiene concern rather than evidence of embedded live credentials.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The placeholders are visible in concrete example configuration. Confidence is moderate because they appear educational and are paired with advice not to commit secret files.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The numerous Ruby or shell backtick findings correspond to Markdown fenced code blocks and inline examples. No executable script, hook, or skill instruction runs these snippets automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are documentation blocks rendered as examples. Review found no executable command wrapper or automation path in the skill files.

low_findings.title: Relative Paths Misclassified as Path Traversal
low_findings.description: The path traversal findings are relative imports and fixed documentation paths such as config file locations. I did not find evidence of user-controlled path construction or file reads performed by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence shows static TypeScript examples and import paths, not active filesystem traversal. No user input reaches these paths in the skill package.

low_findings.title: Environment Access Is Used as Configuration Guidance
low_findings.description: The process.env and config.ini references are part of a guide that discourages direct environment access and recommends centralized configuration. They do not read the evaluator environment or transmit secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious strings occur in instructional examples and anti-pattern sections. There is no executable code in the skill that reads environment variables.

low_findings.title: Network Strings Are Local or Placeholder Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are localhost test commands and a placeholder Sentry DSN. I did not find evidence of real external endpoints used for credential exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network values are visibly placeholders or localhost commands. No code path in the skill performs network requests.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sentry Request Data and Local Variable Collection
dangerous_patterns.description: Documentation recommends Sentry options that may collect request bodies, headers, query strings, user identifiers, and local variables. Teams should disable local variable capture and maintain allowlists or stronger scrubbing before production use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: This is a concrete configuration pattern in the documentation. It is not malicious by itself, but it can expose sensitive data when implemented broadly.

content.value_statement: Backend teams often lose time to inconsistent Express structure, validation, and error handling. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code clear patterns for layered TypeScript services.
actual_capabilities: Explains layered Express architecture with routes, controllers, services, and repositories.
Provides TypeScript examples for controllers, services, Prisma repositories, and middleware.
Guides Zod validation, DTO typing, and validation error handling patterns.
Shows Sentry setup, error capture, spans, and monitoring conventions.
Documents unified configuration patterns and migration away from scattered environment access.
Outlines unit, integration, authentication, and coverage testing practices.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not run migrations, tests, or code generators.
Examples assume Node.js, Express, TypeScript, Prisma, Zod, and Sentry.
Some paths and service names are project-specific and may need adaptation.
Security-sensitive monitoring examples require production redaction review.

use_cases.title: Create a new API feature
use_cases.description: Use the route, controller, service, repository, validation, and testing checklists to structure a new backend feature.

use_cases.title: Refactor legacy Express routes
use_cases.description: Move business logic out of large route files into controllers and services with clearer error handling.

use_cases.title: Standardize service conventions
use_cases.description: Apply shared patterns for Sentry, configuration, middleware, and Prisma access across multiple microservices.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a backend feature
prompt_templates.prompt: Use backend-dev-guidelines to plan a new Express feature for [feature]. List the route, controller, service, repository, validation, and test changes.

prompt_templates.title: Review an endpoint design
prompt_templates.prompt: Use backend-dev-guidelines to review this endpoint design for layered architecture, validation, error handling, Sentry usage, and test coverage: [describe endpoint].

prompt_templates.title: Refactor route logic
prompt_templates.prompt: Use backend-dev-guidelines to refactor this large Express route into controller, service, repository, and Zod validation responsibilities: [paste route summary].

prompt_templates.title: Audit service architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Use backend-dev-guidelines to audit this microservice structure. Identify deviations from the recommended patterns and propose prioritized fixes: [describe files and issues].

output_examples.input: Plan a user profile endpoint using the backend guidelines.
output: A route that registers authentication and audit middleware, then delegates to a controller.
A controller method that validates input, calls a service, and returns a consistent response.
A service method for business rules and a repository method for Prisma access.
Unit and integration test targets for success, validation failure, permission failure, and not found cases.

output_examples.input: Review a route file that contains database queries and validation logic.
output_examples.output: The review would flag business logic in routes, direct Prisma access, missing controller boundaries, and missing Zod schema reuse. It would propose moving database access to a repository and request handling to a controller.

output_examples.input: Standardize monitoring for a backend service.
output_examples.output: The output would recommend early Sentry initialization, scoped error capture, performance spans, health check filtering, and stronger redaction for request data before production.

best_practices: Keep routes focused on paths, middleware, and controller delegation.
Validate all external input with Zod before service logic runs.
Review monitoring and configuration examples for secret and PII redaction before production use.
anti_patterns: Do not put business logic, database queries, or validation branches inside route files.
Do not scatter direct process.env reads across services and controllers.
Do not copy placeholder secrets or broad telemetry settings into production unchanged.

faq.question: Does this skill generate a backend application?
faq.answer: No. It provides architecture guidance, examples, and review criteria for backend code.

faq.question: Which stack does it target?
faq.answer: It targets Node.js, Express, TypeScript, Prisma, Zod, Sentry, and Jest-style testing.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude Code or Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The metadata lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it include security guidance?
faq.answer: It covers validation, authentication middleware, configuration hygiene, and error handling, but production security review is still required.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is documentation only, but some monitoring and secret examples need careful adaptation before production use.

faq.question: When should I use this skill?
faq.answer: Use it when creating, refactoring, or reviewing Express backend routes, controllers, services, repositories, middleware, and tests.
